summary: Gruber catalogs his observations from Apple's October 2005 special event, covering the new iPod with video, the Apple Remote and Front Row software, iTunes 6, and the new iMacs. He notes significant hardware trade-offs like dropping FireWire support and the remote jack in favor of a thinner design. He's skeptical of the $1.99 TV show pricing at 320x240 resolution, pointing out the irony of Apple declaring it 'the year of HD video.' He praises Apple's iPod hardware engineering trajectory and observes that Front Row's full-screen UI is clearly a precursor to Apple's TV ambitions.
key_points: The new iPods dropped FireWire entirely and removed the remote jack, sacrificing peripheral compatibility for a thinner form factor
TV show downloads at $1.99 for 320x240 resolution seem like a poor value proposition, especially given Apple's own 'year of HD video' marketing
Front Row's full-screen media UI is clearly designed for television use, suggesting Apple's incremental strategy toward a media entertainment platform
Jon Rubinstein's move from Mac hardware chief to iPod division head, once seen as a demotion, now looks prescient given the iPod division's extraordinary engineering output
iTunes 5 lasted only one month before being superseded by iTunes 6, an unprecedentedly short version lifecycle
money_quotes: iTunes 5, we hardly knew you. I'm not sure I've ever seen an entire x.0 version number series go by so quickly.
The full-screen UI of Front Row is just begging to be hooked up to a TV. Begging.
Apple's long-term strategy for a media entertainment platform is unfolding incrementally.
$1.99 for each TV show, in only 320 × 240 resolution, doesn't seem like a good deal to me.
Of course, I think ringtones sound like a bad deal, too, but people buy billions of them.
And so much for 'the year of High Definition Video' — 320 × 240 not only isn't HD, it's too low to look good on regular (non-HD) TVs.
I'm sitting here looking at my year-old 40 GB iPod and the thing looks like a relic.
I'm hard-pressed to think of any other hardware company doing as much innovative product design and engineering as Apple's iPod division.
key_insight: Apple's October 2005 event revealed a company making deliberate hardware trade-offs to push thinner designs while incrementally building toward a media entertainment platform, though the initial video offering's low resolution and pricing undercut the vision.
